Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 6
Recent Green phase: Added reproduction (birth) logic — gathered dead neighbors of live cells, kept those with exactly 3 live neighbors. Combined with existing survival filter.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Note: there is now likely duplication / two parallel branches (survival vs birth) that could potentially be unified by iterating over all candidate cells (live + dead-with-live-neighbor) with one predicate.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

Working directory: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-25_09-36-57_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.3-audit-bundle_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking

attachment.content:
- red: TDD Red Phase
- green: TDD Green Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll analyze the current implementation and look for refactoring opportunities.

Now I'll analyze the code carefully.

**Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY):**

- `nextGeneration` — clearly reveals intent
- `cellKey` — clear, produces a string key for a cell
- `NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS` — clear
- `countAliveNeighbors` — clear
- `survivesGeneration` — describes survival rule; but it's actually used for both survival AND birth (both require count==3 to live next gen; survival also allows 2). This name is now slightly misleading since after unification, the predicate could be generalized. Will revisit.
- `aliveKeys`, `isAlive`, `survivors`, `deadCandidates`, `births` — all clear

**Current Mass Calculation:**

Counting components in current implementation:
- Constants: `-1`, `0`, `1` (in NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS, 8 pairs = 16 values, but many repeated), plus `2`, `3`, `3` literals = ~21
- Bindings: many parameters and locals (~30)
- Invocations: ~12 (map, has, filter, set, push, Set constructor, Map constructor, cellKey calls, etc.)
- Conditionals: 3 (`||`, `if !aliveKeys.has`, `if ===3`)
- Loops: 5+ (map, filter, for of, for of inner, for of births, filter inside countAlive)
- Assignments: minimal

The obvious refactoring per the prompt: **unify survival and birth into one pass over all candidate cells (live + dead-with-live-neighbor) with one predicate**. This applies Rule 3 (no duplication) — both branches do the same "count neighbors, apply rule" work.

The unified rule: a cell is alive next gen if `(currently alive AND count in {2,3})` OR `(currently dead AND count == 3)`. This simplifies to: `count == 3 OR (count == 2 AND currently alive)`.

Let me refactor.

content.text:
All 6 tests still pass.

**Refactor Summary**

**Naming Evaluation:**
- `survivesGeneration` removed in favor of `isAliveNextGeneration` — the original name only described the survival branch; the new name accurately reveals the unified rule.
- All other names already reveal intent and were kept.

**APP Mass Calculation (approximate, comparing relative change):**

Before (key components in `nextGeneration` body + `survivesGeneration`):
- Constants: `2`, `3`, `3` literals = 3
- Bindings: aliveKeys, isAlive, survivors, deadCandidates, births, x, y, nx, ny, k, dx, dy ≈ 12
- Invocations: ~14 (Set, map, cellKey×4, has×2, filter, countAliveNeighbors×2, Map, set, push, spread×2)
- Conditionals: 3 (`||`, `if !has`, `if ===3`)
- Loops: 4 (map, filter survivors, for-of cells/offsets ×2, for-of deadCandidates)
- Assignments: ~5 (const declarations counted as bindings; mutations like `set`, `push` count under invocations)

Rough total: 3 + 12 + 28 + 12 + 20 ≈ **75**

After:
- Constants: `3`, `2`, `3` literals = 3
- Bindings: aliveKeys, isAlive, candidates, x, y, nx, ny, dx, dy ≈ 9
- Invocations: ~12 (Set, map, cellKey×4, has, Map, set×2, values, spread, filter, isAlive, countAliveNeighbors, isAliveNextGeneration)
- Conditionals: 2 (`||`, `&&`)
- Loops: 3 (map, for-of cells, for-of offsets, filter) — actually 4
- Assignments: counted as bindings above

Rough total: 3 + 9 + 24 + 8 + 20 ≈ **64**

**Mass change: ~75 → ~64 (Δ ~11 reduction)**

**Rules Applied:**
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): All 6 tests still green
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): `isAliveNextGeneration` better names the unified rule
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): Eliminated the parallel survivor/birth branches — both branches did "count neighbors, apply rule"; now a single filter handles both cases
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Removed the separate `survivors`/`births` collections and the second loop over `deadCandidates`

**Files modified:**
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-25_09-36-57_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.3-audit-bundle_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`

Tests: 6 passing, 2 todo. All green.
